stage.name: PC selection: why the next PC is not always PC plus four
stage.description: A deeper frontend lesson on the next-PC precedence ladder: reset, prediction, default fetch, mispredict correction, replay, exception, return, and commit-PC restart.
stage.courseTemplateName: Professional Technical Deep Dive - Evidence-Grounded SlideKit

scenes.title: The frontend is a PC decision machine

content.title: PC selection: why the next PC is not always PC plus four
content.subtitle: Frontend reading gets serious when PC generation becomes a priority decision, not a simple increment.
content.thesis: The professional question is not "what is the next address?" It is "which source won the next-PC priority ladder, and why?"

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: PC Generation stage
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: PC gen selects the next PC from reset, branch prediction, default fetch, mispredict correction, replay, exception/interrupt, return from exception, or commit-PC restart cases.

content.speakerNotes: Welcome to Unit 04. We are now deep enough to stop treating the frontend as a polite instruction faucet. In CV32A60X, PC generation is a decision machine. Sometimes the next address is the next aligned block. Sometimes it comes from prediction, replay, a resolved mispredict, an exception path, or commit. That priority ladder is the lesson.

actions.text: Welcome to Unit 04. We are now deep enough to stop treating the frontend as a polite instruction faucet. In CV32A60X, PC generation is a decision machine. Sometimes the next address is the next aligned block. Sometimes it comes from prediction, replay, a resolved mispredict, an exception path, or commit. That priority ladder is the lesson.

scenes.title: Read PC gen as a priority ladder

content.title: The official source list is the spine
content.subtitle: PC gen chooses among several sources, and the source tells you what kind of event the core is handling.

steps.title: Reset
steps.body: Boot address wins when the core leaves reset.

steps.title: Prediction or default
steps.body: Use control-flow prediction when available; otherwise fetch the next 32-bit block.

steps.title: Correction
steps.body: A resolved mispredict starts fetching from the correct address.

steps.title: Replay and redirects
steps.body: Queue pressure, exception paths, return-from-exception, and commit-PC restart can override normal flow.

content.takeaway: Every next-PC story should name the winning source before explaining the downstream fetch behavior.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: PC Generation stage
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: PC gen selects the next PC from reset, branch prediction, default fetch, mispredict correction, replay, exception/interrupt, return from exception, or commit-PC restart cases.

content.speakerNotes: Here is the useful mental model: a next-PC source wins a priority contest. Reset is simple. Prediction and default fetching cover the normal fast path. Mispredict feedback is the correction path. Replay, exception, return-from-exception, and commit-PC restart are the cases that separate a real frontend from a toy PC plus four counter.

actions.text: Here is the useful mental model: a next-PC source wins a priority contest. Reset is simple. Prediction and default fetching cover the normal fast path. Mispredict feedback is the correction path. Replay, exception, return-from-exception, and commit-PC restart are the cases that separate a real frontend from a toy PC plus four counter.

content.title: instr_scan gives PC gen control-flow evidence
content.subtitle: The frontend does not wait for the full decode stage before noticing branch-like instructions.

cards.title: instr_scan
cards.body: Pre-decodes branch, jump, return, JALR, call, and immediate facts.

cards.title: RAS path
cards.body: Return-like JALR instructions can use the return address stack.

cards.title: BHT path
cards.body: Conditional branches can use the branch history table direction prediction.

cards.title: Fallback behavior
cards.body: When prediction structure evidence is missing, the source defines fallback or later correction behavior.

content.takeaway: Frontend prediction is source-backed predecode plus a small set of prediction structures.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: instr_scan submodule
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: instr_scan pre-decodes fetched instructions, including compressed instructions, and outputs branch, jump, return, JALR, call, and immediate facts used by branch prediction.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: PC Generation stage
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: PC gen selects the next PC from reset, branch prediction, default fetch, mispredict correction, replay, exception/interrupt, return from exception, or commit-PC restart cases.

content.speakerNotes: A subtle but important point: prediction starts before the instruction reaches the full decode stage. instr_scan pre-decodes enough control-flow information to classify branch, jump, return, JALR, call, and immediate fields. That is why PC generation can talk to RAS or BHT early. It is speculation, yes, but speculation with evidence.

actions.text: A subtle but important point: prediction starts before the instruction reaches the full decode stage. instr_scan pre-decodes enough control-flow information to classify branch, jump, return, JALR, call, and immediate fields. That is why PC generation can talk to RAS or BHT early. It is speculation, yes, but speculation with evidence.

scenes.title: The branch prediction picture is not one box

content.title: Use the diagram to separate roles
content.subtitle: The visual model is useful only if we keep direction, target, return, and correction paths distinct.
content.body: In this unit, the diagram is a reading aid. RAS is for return-address prediction. BHT is for conditional branch direction. BTB is a target-buffer concept, but this configuration has an important caveat that we will handle later.
content.caption: Official branch prediction diagram used as the visual anchor for Unit 04.

callouts.title: Correction
callouts.body: Execute can send resolved branch feedback.

content.takeaway: Never compress all branch prediction behavior into one vague "predictor" sentence.

assets.caption: Official branch prediction diagram from the CV32A60X design documentation.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: PC Generation stage
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: PC gen selects the next PC from reset, branch prediction, default fetch, mispredict correction, replay, exception/interrupt, return from exception, or commit-PC restart cases.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: BHT submodule
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The BHT has 32 entries, stores valid bits and two-bit saturating counters, predicts taken/not taken from the counter MSB, is updated by Execute, and is never flushed.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: RAS submodule
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: RAS is a two-entry LIFO; call JAL pushes the following PC, return JALR pops a predicted return address, wrong prediction can generate a mispredict, and RAS is never flushed.

content.speakerNotes: This diagram is good, but only if we refuse to flatten it. RAS, BHT, target prediction, and correction are different jobs. In this lesson we are building the map. In the next lessons we will zoom into replay, RAS, BHT, and the CV32A60X BTB caveat. Yes, the caveat matters. Hardware is where footnotes become bugs.

actions.text: This diagram is good, but only if we refuse to flatten it. RAS, BHT, target prediction, and correction are different jobs. In this lesson we are building the map. In the next lessons we will zoom into replay, RAS, BHT, and the CV32A60X BTB caveat. Yes, the caveat matters. Hardware is where footnotes become bugs.

scenes.title: Prediction and correction are different moments

content.title: Do not mix the guess with the repair
content.subtitle: A clean explanation separates candidate PC selection from later validation.

columns.title: Frontend guess
columns.body: PC gen uses predecode and prediction structures to choose a candidate next PC before the instruction is fully resolved.

columns.title: Execute correction
columns.body: When a branch is resolved later, mispredict feedback can redirect fetching to the correct address.

content.takeaway: The frontend is allowed to guess, but the pipeline must be able to repair the guess.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: PC Generation stage
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: PC gen selects the next PC from reset, branch prediction, default fetch, mispredict correction, replay, exception/interrupt, return from exception, or commit-PC restart cases.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: Fetch stage
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: Fetch requests 32-bit aligned blocks from cache, realigns instructions, pushes entries into the instruction queue, attaches predicted addresses, and forwards cache exceptions to decode.

content.speakerNotes: A professional frontend explanation separates two moments. First, the frontend chooses a candidate PC and fetches around that guess. Later, execute resolves a branch and can report a mispredict. If those two moments blur together, students start thinking prediction is magic. It is not magic. It is a fast guess plus a repair contract.

actions.text: A professional frontend explanation separates two moments. First, the frontend chooses a candidate PC and fetches around that guess. Later, execute resolves a branch and can report a mispredict. If those two moments blur together, students start thinking prediction is magic. It is not magic. It is a fast guess plus a repair contract.

content.title: Five questions for every frontend PC claim
content.subtitle: Use this checklist before turning source text into a slide or a technical explanation.

items.title: Which source won?
items.body: Reset, prediction, default, mispredict, replay, exception, return, or commit PC?

items.title: Who produced the evidence?
items.body: instr_scan, RAS, BHT, execute, CSR, controller, or commit?

items.title: Was this a guess or a correction?
items.body: Prediction and resolved feedback belong to different moments.

items.title: What was sent to fetch?
items.body: Fetch uses the selected address stream to request and realign instruction data.

items.title: What must be flushed or replayed?
items.body: The answer decides whether old queued work can still be trusted.

content.takeaway: This checklist is how we keep Unit 04 technical without letting the explanation sprawl.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: PC Generation stage
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: PC gen selects the next PC from reset, branch prediction, default fetch, mispredict correction, replay, exception/interrupt, return from exception, or commit-PC restart cases.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: Fetch stage
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: Fetch requests 32-bit aligned blocks from cache, realigns instructions, pushes entries into the instruction queue, attaches predicted addresses, and forwards cache exceptions to decode.

content.speakerNotes: Use this checklist every time you read a frontend claim. Which source won the PC decision? Who produced the evidence? Was the source a guess or a correction? What did fetch do with that address? And what old work must be flushed or replayed? These five questions keep the lesson precise and prevent the classic handwave: the frontend predicts branches.

actions.text: Use this checklist every time you read a frontend claim. Which source won the PC decision? Who produced the evidence? Was the source a guess or a correction? What did fetch do with that address? And what old work must be flushed or replayed? These five questions keep the lesson precise and prevent the classic handwave: the frontend predicts branches.

questions.question: Which statement is the best professional summary of PC generation in this lesson?

options.label: It chooses the next PC from a prioritized set of normal, predictive, replay, and redirect sources
It only increments the PC by four after every instruction
It decodes every instruction completely before fetching anything
It belongs to commit, not the frontend

questions.analysis: The source lists several next-PC origins; PC plus four or default fetch is only one part of the story.

questions.question: Which items can affect the next-PC path according to the CV32A60X frontend source?
questions.analysis: All four are listed as next-PC sources or redirect-related sources in the PC generation/frontend material.

questions.question: What does instr_scan contribute to frontend prediction?

options.label: It pre-decodes control-flow facts such as branch, jump, return, JALR, call, and immediate fields
It updates the architectural register file

questions.analysis: instr_scan provides early control-flow classification that branch prediction uses before full decode.

questions.question: Why should prediction and correction be explained separately?

options.label: Prediction is a frontend guess, while correction comes from later resolved information such as execute feedback
They are the same event with two names
Prediction is only used by the commit stage

questions.analysis: The lesson separates candidate selection from later branch resolution or redirect behavior.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: PC Generation stage
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: PC gen selects the next PC from reset, branch prediction, default fetch, mispredict correction, replay, exception/interrupt, return from exception, or commit-PC restart cases.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: instr_scan submodule
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: instr_scan pre-decodes fetched instructions, including compressed instructions, and outputs branch, jump, return, JALR, call, and immediate facts used by branch prediction.

content.speakerNotes: These questions check whether the mental model is already precise. If you can name the source of the next PC, explain instr_scan, and separate prediction from correction, the rest of Unit 04 will feel much less mysterious. If not, pause here, because RAS, BHT, BTB, replay, and redirect are just specialized versions of this same decision discipline.

actions.text: These questions check whether the mental model is already precise. If you can name the source of the next PC, explain instr_scan, and separate prediction from correction, the rest of Unit 04 will feel much less mysterious. If not, pause here, because RAS, BHT, BTB, replay, and redirect are just specialized versions of this same decision discipline.

scenes.title: PC selection is now a readable problem

content.title: You can now read the frontend without reducing it to PC plus four
content.subtitle: A next-PC explanation should name the winning source and the evidence behind it.
takeaways: PC gen chooses among normal, predictive, replay, and redirect sources.
instr_scan gives early control-flow evidence to prediction logic.
Prediction is a guess; execute, CSR, controller, and commit paths can correct or redirect.
content.nextStep: Next: replay, return, exception, and redirect cases, where the frontend has to recover gracefully.
content.closing: Frontend quality is not just fetching fast. It is knowing when the fast answer is no longer safe.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: PC Generation stage
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: PC gen selects the next PC from reset, branch prediction, default fetch, mispredict correction, replay, exception/interrupt, return from exception, or commit-PC restart cases.

content.speakerNotes: You now have the core habit for Unit 04. The next PC is not a number floating in space. It is the output of a priority decision, backed by a source of evidence. Next we study the less ordinary paths: replay, return, exception, and redirect. That is where frontend design starts showing its real engineering character.

actions.text: You now have the core habit for Unit 04. The next PC is not a number floating in space. It is the output of a priority decision, backed by a source of evidence.
